content.text: What Trust Calibration Research Measures

Trust calibration research determines whether users' trust in a GenAI system matches the system's actual reliability. Two failure modes exist: under-trust (users verify every AI output regardless of quality, eliminating the efficiency gain) and over-trust or automation bias (users accept AI output without meaningful verification, propagating errors undetected). Both have measurable consequences: under-trust produces no ROI; over-trust produces quality failures and regulatory exposure.

Trust calibration research is distinct from general user research: it deliberately manipulates the quality of AI outputs to observe verification behaviour, rather than asking users to report how they would behave. Stated trust and observed trust frequently diverge — users report they 'always check' and then accept erroneous output in 40–60% of cases under time pressure.

attrs.alt: Trust calibration spectrum with four research methods mapped to detection goals

content.text: The Trust Calibration Spectrum
Trust level
Observable behaviour
Consequence for GenAI feature
Distrust / Resistance
Ignores AI output; re-enters data from source; takes 3× longer than baseline
No efficiency gain; potential for workflow abandonment after launch
Under-trust
Uses AI but verifies every field including low-risk ones; frustrated by extra step
Partial efficiency gain; user fatigue; risk of reverting to non-AI workflow
Calibrated trust
Verifies high-stakes fields; trusts AI on routine; catches errors in high-consequence areas
Full efficiency gain; quality maintained; sustainable long-term adoption
Over-trust
Accepts AI output after a cursory glance; rarely cross-references source
Efficiency gain achieved; quality at risk; errors propagate without correction
Automation bias
Trusts AI output over own correct prior judgement; overrides accurate human intuition
Errors actively suppressed; regulatory / legal risk; most dangerous state
Research Method 1: Calibration Interview

The calibration interview shows participants a real AI output for their domain and observes how they interact with it. Unlike a standard usability session, the focus is on the verification process: what does the user check, in what order, and how long before they act on the output?
Prepare 4–6 AI outputs from the actual model for realistic domain tasks. Include 2–3 clean outputs and 1–2 outputs with seeded errors (without telling the participant).
Present each output and ask the participant to process it as they normally would. Do not give time pressure — natural behaviour is the goal.
Observe and annotate: which sections does the user read? What do they cross-reference? Do they open the original document?
After they finish: 'Walk me through what you just did.' This surfaces verification steps they did not verbalise.
Ask: 'Is there anything in this output you're unsure about?' — surfaces latent doubt that verification behaviour did not show.
Research Method 2: Error Injection Study

The error injection study is the most direct method for measuring automation bias. Participants are given AI outputs with planted errors of known severity. The study measures the catch rate: what fraction of participants notice each error type.

# Error injection study: design for FNOL chatbot

ERROR_SET = [
    {
        "error_id": "E01",
        "error_type": "severity_understatement",
        "description": "AI summary says 'minor vehicle damage' when submission describes 'total loss'",
        "severity": "critical",   # incorrect field that routes claim to wrong team
        "expected_catch_rate": 0.90,  # researchers expect 90% of users to notice
    },
    {
        "error_id": "E02",
        "error_type": "date_transposition",
        "description": "AI records incident date as 14 June instead of 16 June",
        "severity": "moderate",
        "expected_catch_rate": 0.50,
    },
    {
        "error_id": "E03",
        "error_type": "policy_coverage_error",
        "description": "AI states 'comprehensive cover applies' when policy has third-party only",
        "severity": "critical",
        "expected_catch_rate": 0.85,
    },
    {
        "error_id": "E04",
        "error_type": "plausible_fabrication",
        "description": "AI invents a third party vehicle registration not mentioned by claimant",
        "severity": "critical",
        "expected_catch_rate": 0.60,   # low — data looks plausible
    },
]

# Study design
STUDY_DESIGN = {
    "n_participants":    12,
    "tasks_per_person":  4,   # each person sees 2 error tasks + 2 clean tasks
    "counterbalancing":  "Latin square — each error type seen by 3 participants",
    "instruction":       "Process this FNOL claim as you normally would",
    "what_to_observe":   [
        "Does the participant read the full AI output or skim?",
        "Which sections do they cross-reference against the original submission?",
        "Do they notice the error without prompting?",
        "If they notice: do they correct it or flag it to a colleague?",
        "If they miss it: what were they doing instead?",
    ],
}

The catch rate by error type directly drives the verification UI requirements. An error type with a catch rate below 60% in the study will produce the same (or lower) catch rate in production, because production users are under more time pressure than study participants. Any error type with a production consequence classification of 'critical' and a study catch rate below 80% requires a mandatory UI verification checkpoint.
Research Method 3: Expectation Survey

The expectation survey measures the population-level distribution of trust expectations before launch. It translates qualitative interview signals into quantitative thresholds that can inform NFR setting and the accuracy communication strategy.

The survey is most useful when run with 40+ participants across multiple roles and experience levels. The distribution (not just the mean) matters: a bimodal distribution (half expect 90% accuracy, half expect 50%) indicates sub-populations with incompatible mental models that require different UX approaches or training programmes.
Research Method 4: Shadow Observation

Shadow observation watches users interact with a deployed or near-deployed AI system in their real work context. It is the only method that captures actual verification behaviour under real conditions — time pressure, interruptions, and task load — rather than study conditions. Stated trust and observed trust diverge most under real conditions.

# Shadow observation protocol: structured observation form

OBSERVATION_FORM = """
Session: {session_id}
Participant: {participant_id} | Role: {role}
Task: {task_description}
AI output quality: [ ] Error present: {error_id} | [ ] Clean output

OBSERVATION CHECKLIST:
□ Did participant read AI output fully before acting?
□ Which sections did participant cross-reference? ________________
□ Did participant verify against source document (original claim submission)? [ ] Yes [ ] No
□ Did participant catch the planted error (if present)? [ ] Yes [ ] No [ ] N/A
□ Time from seeing AI output to acting on it: _______ seconds
□ Did participant express verbal uncertainty ('let me check this')? [ ] Yes [ ] No
□ Did participant make any changes to the AI output? [ ] Yes [ ] No
   If yes, what did they change? ________________

AUTOMATION BIAS INDICATORS (check all observed):
□ Accepted AI category without checking original submission
□ Used AI confidence language verbatim without independent verification
□ Did not read section with planted error
□ Submitted AI output within 10 seconds of viewing

UNDER-TRUST INDICATORS:
□ Checked every field against original even on clean outputs
□ Expressed verbal distrust ('I never trust these')
□ Re-entered data from original rather than using AI pre-fill

POST-TASK QUESTION (ask verbatim):
'How confident were you in the AI's summary for that claim?'
'Was there anything you were unsure about?'
"""

Shadow observation is most valuable post-launch or in a controlled pilot with 10–20 users. The observation protocol above uses a structured form to ensure consistent data collection across observers. Key comparisons: error-present vs clean task sessions, experienced vs new users, high-volume vs low-volume periods.
Dataclasses: Trust Calibration Study

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class TrustLevel(str, Enum):
    DISTRUST          = "distrust"       # refuses to use AI; always overrides
    UNDER_TRUST       = "under_trust"    # uses AI but verifies every output exhaustively
    CALIBRATED        = "calibrated"     # verifies when stakes are high; trusts on routine
    OVER_TRUST        = "over_trust"     # accepts AI output without meaningful verification
    AUTOMATION_BIAS   = "automation_bias" # trusts AI over own correct judgement

@dataclass
class TrustObservation:
    participant_id: str
    task: str
    ai_output_had_error: bool
    participant_caught_error: bool
    verification_steps_taken: list[str]
    override_decision: Optional[str]  # what the participant changed, if anything
    stated_trust: int                 # self-report 1–5
    observed_trust: TrustLevel        # researcher classification from behaviour

    def trust_calibrated(self) -> bool:
        """
        True if the participant caught errors when present and trusted when correct.
        False if they missed an error (over-trust) or spent excessive time checking
        an error-free output (under-trust).
        """
        if self.ai_output_had_error:
            return self.participant_caught_error
        no_error_and_quick = len(self.verification_steps_taken) <= 2
        return no_error_and_quick

@dataclass
class TrustCalibrationStudy:
    feature_name: str
    observations: list[TrustObservation] = field(default_factory=list)

    def automation_bias_rate(self) -> float:
        """Fraction of error-seeded outputs accepted without correction."""
        error_tasks = [o for o in self.observations if o.ai_output_had_error]
        if not error_tasks:
            return 0.0
        missed = [o for o in error_tasks if not o.participant_caught_error]
        return len(missed) / len(error_tasks)

    def under_trust_rate(self) -> float:
        """Fraction of clean outputs with excessive verification (> 2 steps)."""
        clean_tasks = [o for o in self.observations if not o.ai_output_had_error]
        if not clean_tasks:
            return 0.0
        over_checked = [o for o in clean_tasks if len(o.verification_steps_taken) > 2]
        return len(over_checked) / len(clean_tasks)

    def calibration_score(self) -> float:
        """Fraction of observations showing calibrated trust behaviour."""
        calibrated = [o for o in self.observations if o.trust_calibrated()]
        return len(calibrated) / len(self.observations) if self.observations else 0.0
Calibration Interventions by Trust Level

Research findings map directly to UX and training interventions. The two failure modes — automation bias and distrust — require different responses. The `TrustCalibrationStudy.automation_bias_rate()` and `under_trust_rate()` results from the error injection study drive which interventions are prioritised for launch.
Mapping Research Findings to Feature Requirements
Research finding
Requirement type
Requirement example
Automation bias rate > 30% on critical-severity errors
Functional requirement
UI must display mandatory confirmation step before submitting critical fields (severity, coverage)
Under-trust rate > 40% on clean outputs
Functional requirement
Side-by-side comparison view required; real-time accuracy statistics displayed per user
Expected accuracy mean > 4.0 (over-expectation)
Communication requirement
Accuracy onboarding: display real system accuracy stats on first use; weekly accuracy digest
Acceptable error rate median ≤ 1 in 10
NFR (accuracy)
Hallucination rate NFR tightened to ≤ 5% (from initial 10% draft)
Verification checklist items with < 40% intention to verify
UX requirement
Those fields require explicit AI confidence badge and are highlighted for review
Trust drops sharply after first caught error
Communication requirement
Error correction flow must explain what happened and provide accuracy context
Pilot Deployment as Trust Research

A controlled pilot with 10–20 users is the highest-fidelity trust calibration method available before full launch. It runs the actual system with real data under supervised conditions, using shadow observation to record trust behaviour. The pilot produces three artefacts:
Automation bias rate by error type and user segment — determines which verification checkpoints are mandatory at launch
Verification behaviour map by workflow step — identifies which steps users bypass and which they over-check
Trust trajectory over time — reveals whether trust calibrates naturally over the first 2–4 weeks or requires intervention

A pilot finding of automation bias rate > 40% on any critical-severity error type is a launch blocker. The feature must be redesigned with mandatory verification for that error type before rollout to the full user population.
Common Mistakes in Trust Calibration Research
Mistake
Consequence
Correct practice
Using stated trust as a proxy for observed trust
Overestimates calibration; under-designs verification UX
Always measure verification behaviour, not just self-report
Only showing error-free AI outputs in research
Cannot measure automation bias; misses the most important failure mode
Seed at least 30% of research tasks with planted errors
Planting only obvious errors
Inflates catch rate; leads to over-confident verification design
Include plausible errors (dates, vehicle details) that require source cross-reference to detect
Ignoring time pressure in study design
Study catch rates are 20–30% higher than production catch rates
Run at least some sessions with realistic time pressure (queue of 5 tasks)
No trust calibration research before launch
Trust failures discovered after rollout; reputational and regulatory consequences
Run error injection study (n=12) before any production launch
Summary

Trust calibration research determines whether users' actual verification behaviour matches the GenAI system's real reliability. It uses four methods — calibration interviews, error injection studies, expectation surveys, and shadow observation — each suited to different stages and questions. The error injection study is the most critical: it directly measures automation bias by seeding real AI outputs with planted errors and observing catch rates. Catch rates below 80% on critical-severity errors require mandatory UI verification checkpoints before launch. Stated trust consistently overestimates observed trust; only behavioural observation under realistic conditions (including time pressure) produces reliable data for feature design and NFR setting.
